none: I-55 from Woodrow Wilson Dr to I-220, Jackson section : environmental impact statement.

The project involves upgrading a section of Interstate 55 in Jackson, Mississippi, from Woodrow Wilson Drive to Interstate 220. The aim is to improve traffic flow and safety by expanding the highway to a six-lane divided facility. This will require additional land and the relocation of some families and businesses. The project is expected to reduce congestion and pollution, enhance economic benefits, and improve access for pedestrians and cyclists. However, it will also have adverse effects, including noise impacts on nearby residences and the loss of some visual buffers.
I-55 from Woodrow Wilson Dr to I-220, Jackson section : environmental impact statement.

The project involves upgrading a section of Interstate 55 in Jackson, Mississippi, from Woodrow Wilson Drive to Interstate 220. The aim is to improve traffic flow and safety by expanding the highway to a six-lane divided facility. This will require additional land and the relocation of some families and businesses. The project is expected to reduce congestion and pollution, enhance economic benefits, and improve access for pedestrians and cyclists. However, it will also have adverse effects, including noise impacts on nearby residences and the loss of some visual buffers.
Jackson, Hinds and Madison Counties, Mississippi

The I-55 upgrade project in Jackson was proposed to address increasing traffic congestion and improve safety along a critical transportation route. While the project aimed to enhance economic benefits and accessibility, it faced public pushback due to concerns over displacement of families and businesses, as well as potential environmental impacts. Ultimately, the project moved forward with plans to mitigate some of the adverse effects.

"The proposed design has been developed through consultation with officials of the City of Jackson and has their approval. The proposal will have a positive effect on the park in that it will provide safe and efficient access." — City of Jackson (Comments from the Planning Director)
"The measures outlined to minimize harm to the park appear to be sufficient." — U.S. Department of Interior
